q: Coffee as a drink was first developed in which country, before spreading to the Arabian Peninsula?
opts: Yemen
Ethiopia
Egypt
Sudan
explain: Coffee cherries were first consumed in Ethiopia, but brewing coffee as a drink was developed in Yemen around the 15th century. Sufi monks used it to stay awake during night prayers.

q: What is the cherry-like fruit of the coffee plant called?
opts: Coffee berry
Coffee cherry
Coffee drupe
Coffee pod
explain: The fruit is called a coffee cherry. Each cherry typically contains two seeds (the coffee beans) facing each other. Occasionally one seed aborts and the other grows round — this is called a peaberry.

q: What is the SCA recommended brewing temperature range for specialty coffee?
explain: 90–96°C is the SCA standard. Light roasts benefit from the higher end (93–96°C) to fully extract complex compounds, while darker roasts work better slightly lower to avoid bitterness.

q: What is the minimum SCA cupping score for a coffee to be classified as specialty grade?
opts: 75 points
80 points
85 points
90 points
explain: 80+ out of 100 on the SCA cupping form earns specialty classification. 85+ is considered excellent, 90+ outstanding. Most top Czech specialty roasters source 84–88 point coffees.

q: In natural (dry) processing, what makes the coffee taste fruitier and heavier?
opts: Special bacteria added during drying
The fruit pulp dries on the bean, fermenting sugars into the seed
The beans are soaked in fruit juice
Higher roasting temperatures used for naturals
explain: Natural processing leaves the fruit intact around the bean while it dries. The sugars and yeasts in the fruit ferment into the bean over weeks, producing fruity, wine-like, heavy-bodied flavours.

q: What does 'washed process' (wet process) mean?
opts: Beans are brewed with filtered water
Fruit pulp is removed before drying, producing cleaner flavour
Beans are washed with chemicals to remove defects
Water is added during roasting
explain: Washed coffees have the fruit removed mechanically before drying. This produces cleaner, brighter cups with more pronounced acidity and terroir — the bean's origin character shines through without fruit interference.

q: What is honey process coffee?
opts: Coffee with honey added after brewing
Coffee where some mucilage (sticky fruit layer) is left on the bean during drying
Coffee dried in beehives for natural flavouring
A blend of washed and natural process beans
explain: Honey process is between washed and natural. The skin is removed but varying amounts of mucilage (the sticky honey-like layer) remain on the bean. Yellow/red/black honey refers to how much mucilage is left — more = fruitier, heavier body.

q: Why does altitude matter for coffee quality?
opts: Higher altitude = more rainfall = more nutrients
Higher altitude = cooler temperatures = slower cherry development = more complex sugars
Higher altitude = more UV exposure = darker roast needed
Higher altitude = less oxygen = easier harvesting
explain: At high altitude (1500–2200m), cooler temperatures slow cherry ripening dramatically. This extended development time allows more complex sugars and acids to develop. Ethiopian Chelbesa at ~2000m is a perfect example — the jasmine and bergamot notes come partly from altitude.

q: What is CO₂ degassing and why does it matter for brewing?
opts: CO₂ is a flavour compound that improves taste
Freshly roasted beans release CO₂ that can disrupt extraction if not allowed to escape first
CO₂ is produced during grinding and must be removed
CO₂ buildup causes espresso to be under-extracted
explain: Roasting produces CO₂ inside beans. For 7–14 days after roasting, beans off-gas CO₂. Brewing too fresh means CO₂ escapes during extraction, creating uneven flow. This is why the bloom phase matters — it lets CO₂ escape before the main brew.

q: What is the main chemical compound responsible for coffee's bitterness?
opts: Caffeine
Chlorogenic acids
Trigonelline
Melanoidins
explain: Chlorogenic acids break down during roasting into quinic acids and other bitter compounds. Caffeine also contributes some bitterness but less than commonly thought. Melanoidins (from Maillard reaction) add more bitterness in darker roasts.

q: What are the two main commercial coffee species?
opts: Arabica and Liberica
Arabica and Robusta
Coffea canephora and Coffea liberica
Typica and Bourbon
explain: Arabica (Coffea arabica) accounts for ~60% of global production — higher quality, more complex, grown at altitude. Robusta (Coffea canephora) is hardier, higher caffeine (~2.7% vs 1.5%), more bitter. Most specialty coffee is Arabica.

q: What does 'single origin' mean on a coffee bag?
opts: Coffee from one single farm
Coffee from one country, region, or specific farm — traceable to its source
Coffee processed using only one method
Coffee from a single harvest year
explain: Single origin means the coffee is traceable to a specific geographic source — a country, region, cooperative, or single farm. This contrasts with blends which mix beans from multiple origins to create consistency.

q: What coffee variety is Kenya famous for, known for blackcurrant and complex acidity?
opts: Gesha
Typica
SL28 and SL34
Catuai
explain: SL28 and SL34 (Scott Laboratories selections) are Kenya's iconic varieties. They produce the characteristic blackcurrant, tomato acidity and heavy body. Your Pikolo Kenya Kainamui AA was this variety.

q: What flavour profile is Colombia most known for in specialty coffee?
opts: Earthy, herbal, full body
Balanced, caramel sweetness, mild acidity, red fruit
Floral, tea-like, bergamot
Heavy fruit, wine-like, fermented notes
explain: Colombian specialty coffee is typically well-balanced — caramel sweetness, mild-medium acidity, red fruit (apple, cherry), and clean finish. The Huila and Nariño regions tend more floral; Antioquia more chocolatey.

q: Ethiopia is often called the birthplace of coffee. Which Ethiopian region is famous for jasmine and bergamot notes?
opts: Sidama
Yirgacheffe / Gedeo Zone
Harrar
Jimma
explain: Yirgacheffe in the Gedeo Zone produces some of the world's most aromatic coffees — jasmine, bergamot, white tea. The high altitude (1700–2200m), washed processing, and heirloom varieties combine to produce this unique profile.

q: Costa Rica is famous for which processing innovation?
opts: Anaerobic fermentation
Honey process — developed and refined here
Wet-hulled process
Cold brew processing
explain: Costa Rica pioneered and refined honey processing. The country banned natural processing for decades (quality control) but developed sophisticated honey process variants. This is why Costa Rican coffees often have beautiful sweetness and clean fruit character.

q: What is anaerobic fermentation in coffee processing?
opts: Coffee fermented in open tanks with oxygen
Coffee sealed in oxygen-free tanks during fermentation, producing intense fruit and funky flavours
Coffee fermented underwater
A natural drying technique requiring no water
explain: Anaerobic processing seals cherries or beans in tanks with no oxygen. This forces different fermentation pathways, producing intense tropical fruit, wine-like, sometimes unusual flavours. Your Tarbon A.I was anaerobic — that's where the banana and tropical notes came from.

q: What is 'wet-hulled' (Giling Basah) processing, typically associated with Sumatra?
opts: Beans washed multiple times before drying
Parchment removed while beans are still wet, creating earthy, heavy body
Beans dried fully before hulling
A traditional Japanese drying method
explain: Wet-hulling is unique to Indonesia. The parchment layer is removed while beans still have high moisture content (unlike other processes). This produces Sumatra's characteristic earthy, heavy, low-acid profile with herbal and mushroom notes.

q: What happens to acidity during roasting?
opts: Acidity increases as beans get darker
Acidity decreases as roast level increases
Acidity stays constant regardless of roast
Acidity increases only in the first crack stage
explain: Chlorogenic acids degrade during roasting, reducing perceived acidity. This is why light roasts taste bright and acidic while dark roasts taste flat or bitter. The complex fruit acids in specialty light roasts are largely lost by medium-dark.

q: What is 'first crack' in coffee roasting?
opts: The first inspection opening of the roasting drum
An audible pop as beans expand and moisture escapes
The sound of the cooling fan starting
When the outer chaff layer separates from the bean
explain: First crack occurs when steam and CO₂ pressure inside the bean exceeds the cell wall strength — the bean physically breaks open. It sounds like popcorn popping. Light roasts are pulled just after first crack; second crack signals medium-dark territory.

q: What is the Maillard reaction in coffee roasting?
opts: The process of extracting caffeine
A chemical reaction between sugars and amino acids that creates hundreds of flavour compounds and brown colour
The process that produces CO₂ during roasting
The reaction that destroys chlorogenic acids
explain: The Maillard reaction is responsible for the complex flavours in roasted coffee — caramel, chocolate, nutty, roasty notes. The same reaction browns bread and meat. It begins around 150°C and continues through the roast, creating over 800 flavour compounds.

q: Robusta has roughly double the caffeine of Arabica. What percentage does Robusta contain?
explain: Robusta contains ~2.7% caffeine vs Arabica's ~1.5%. Caffeine is a natural insecticide — growing at lower altitudes with more pests, Robusta evolved higher caffeine as protection. The higher caffeine also contributes to Robusta's harsher, more bitter flavour.

q: Which of these is NOT in the 'Bean Belt' (the equatorial zone where coffee grows)?
opts: Brazil
Colombia
Germany
Vietnam
explain: The Bean Belt is the equatorial zone between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn — where coffee plants thrive. Germany is far outside this zone. Brazil (largest producer), Colombia, Vietnam, and Ethiopia are all within it.

q: What is a 'peaberry' coffee?
opts: Coffee from small farms only
A single round bean that forms when only one seed develops in the cherry
A defective bean with a pea-like smell
The first beans harvested in the season
explain: Normally a coffee cherry contains two flat-sided beans. A peaberry forms when only one seed develops, growing round instead of flat. They're considered sweeter and more intense — the nutrients meant for two beans concentrate in one. About 5% of any crop.

q: What is the 'third wave' of coffee?
opts: The third crop harvest of the year
A movement treating coffee as artisanal, focusing on origin, processing, and transparent sourcing
The third brewing method (after espresso and filter)
The third generation of espresso machines
explain: Third wave coffee (from ~2000s) treats coffee like wine — emphasising terroir, processing method, variety, and the craft of brewing. First wave = mass commodity coffee. Second wave = Starbucks/café culture. Third wave = specialty, single origin, pour over culture.

q: What does 'direct trade' mean in specialty coffee?
opts: Coffee traded directly between countries without import tariffs
Roasters buying directly from farmers, often at above-market prices with direct relationship
Coffee sold directly to consumers without a shop
Trade certified by a direct trade organisation
explain: Direct trade means the roaster has a direct relationship with the farmer — visiting farms, paying above Fair Trade prices, and providing feedback on quality. This contrasts with commodity trading through brokers. Many of your roasters (Kmen, DOT, Mia) practice direct or relationship trade.

q: How much caffeine does a standard double espresso contain approximately?
explain: A standard double espresso (60ml) contains roughly 80–120mg of caffeine. Interestingly, a filter coffee (240ml) contains 80–200mg — often more than espresso because of the larger volume of water extracting over longer time.

q: Which country produces the famous Gesha (Geisha) variety, originally from Ethiopia?
opts: Jamaica
Hawaii
Panama
Colombia
explain: Panama's Hacienda La Esmeralda 'discovered' and popularised Gesha (traced to Gesha village, Ethiopia) in the early 2000s. It now fetches record auction prices. Characteristics: intense jasmine, bergamot, peach, tropical fruit — one of the world's most prized coffees.

q: What is extraction yield in coffee brewing?
opts: The total volume of coffee brewed
The percentage of coffee solids dissolved from the grounds into the cup
The ratio of water to coffee used
The time taken to extract the coffee
explain: Extraction yield is the percentage of dry coffee mass that dissolved into the brew. SCA recommends 18–22% for optimal flavour. Under 18% = under-extracted (sour, salty, weak). Over 22% = over-extracted (bitter, dry, hollow).

q: What is TDS (Total Dissolved Solids) in coffee?
opts: The weight of the coffee grounds used
The concentration of coffee compounds in the final brew, measured in %
The total water dissolved during brewing
The dissolved mineral content of the brew water
explain: TDS measures how much coffee material is dissolved in the water — essentially, how strong the coffee is. SCA recommends 1.15–1.35% TDS for filter coffee. Measured with a refractometer, it can calculate extraction yield when combined with brew ratio.

q: In espresso, what does a sour, acidic shot typically indicate?
opts: Over-extraction
Under-extraction
Correct extraction with high-acid beans
Temperature too high
explain: Under-extraction means water passed through too quickly, dissolving only the easily-extracted acidic compounds without reaching the sweeter ones. Fix: grind finer, increase dose, raise temperature, or extend time.

q: In espresso, what does a bitter, harsh, dry shot typically indicate?
opts: Under-extraction
Over-extraction
Correct extraction — some bitterness is normal
Grind too coarse
explain: Over-extraction means water dissolved too many compounds including the bitter ones. Fix: grind coarser, reduce dose, lower temperature, or reduce time. A well-extracted shot should be sweet, balanced, with pleasant acidity and only background bitterness.

q: What is 'channeling' in espresso and what causes it?
opts: The groove cut in the portafilter basket
Water exploiting weak paths through the puck, causing uneven extraction
The flow channel in the group head
Overflow from the portafilter during extraction
explain: Channeling occurs when water finds the path of least resistance through the puck — often from uneven tamping, poor distribution, or a cracked puck. That channel over-extracts while the rest under-extracts. WDT tool, distribution, and proper tamping prevent it.

q: Why is the bloom phase essential in pour over brewing?
opts: It pre-heats the dripper
CO₂ released from fresh beans would disrupt even extraction if not degassed first
It dissolves the fines to prevent clogging
It activates enzymes in the paper filter
explain: Fresh beans are saturated with CO₂. Pouring bloom water (2–3× coffee weight) allows CO₂ to degas before the main brew. Without blooming, CO₂ escapes during extraction, creating channels and uneven flow. 30–45 seconds is typical.

q: What is the standard SCA recommended ratio for filter/pour over coffee?
explain: The SCA Golden Cup standard is 55g coffee per litre of water — approximately 1:15 to 1:18. You brew at 1:15 (20g/300g) which is on the stronger end of the range, producing a more concentrated, flavourful cup.

q: Finer grinding in espresso does what to shot time and extraction?
opts: Faster shot, less extraction
Slower shot, more extraction
Faster shot, more extraction
Same shot time, more extraction
explain: Finer grind = smaller particles = more surface area = more resistance to water flow = slower shot. More contact time + more surface area = more extraction. This is why grinding finer fixes under-extracted sour shots.

q: Higher water temperature in espresso generally does what?
opts: Extracts less, producing sweeter shots
Extracts more, which can improve light roasts but over-extract darker ones
Has no effect on extraction
Reduces crema production
explain: Higher temperature increases extraction rate and yield. Light roasts benefit from higher temps (93–96°C) because their compounds are harder to dissolve. Darker roasts at high temperature quickly over-extract bitter compounds — drop to 88–91°C.

q: What is tamping pressure supposed to achieve?
opts: The harder you tamp the better — more pressure = better espresso
Create a uniform, level, dense puck that water flows through evenly
Remove air pockets from the coffee bed
Add pressure to help the pump extract better
explain: Tamping creates a uniform puck with consistent density so water flows evenly through all the coffee. The specific pressure (30lbs is often cited) matters less than consistency and levelness. Calibrated tampers (like your Ikape V6) click at a set pressure to ensure repeatability.

q: What is the standard espresso extraction time range?
opts: 10–15 seconds
20–30 seconds
35–45 seconds
45–60 seconds
explain: 20–30 seconds for a double espresso is the traditional range, though modern specialty espresso often extends to 25–35 seconds. Your shots at 27–32 seconds are well within the sweet spot for specialty light roasts.

q: What does a 1:2 espresso ratio mean?
opts: 1 gram of water per 2 grams of coffee
2 grams of espresso output for every 1 gram of coffee input
Equal parts coffee and water
1 part espresso to 2 parts milk
explain: Espresso ratio is expressed as dose:yield. 1:2 means 18g coffee produces 36g espresso. 1:1.5 is ristretto (shorter, more intense). 1:3+ is lungo (longer, more dilute). Most specialty espresso targets 1:2 to 1:2.5.

q: What is WDT (Weiss Distribution Technique)?
opts: A method of tamping at an angle
Stirring the grounds in the portafilter with a thin needle tool to break up clumps
A water temperature calibration method
A technique for distributing water during pour over
explain: WDT uses a thin needle or multi-needle tool to stir coffee grounds in the portafilter basket before tamping. This breaks up clumps (which cause channeling) and creates a more uniform density puck. Significantly improves espresso consistency.

q: In pour over, what does pouring higher and faster generally do?
opts: Less agitation, cleaner cup
More agitation of the coffee bed, increasing extraction and body
Reduces extraction time only
Has no effect on flavour
explain: Higher, faster pours create more turbulence in the coffee bed, agitating the grounds and increasing extraction. You experienced this directly with the Kenya Kainamui — higher pours with more agitation dramatically improved the flavour by extracting more fully.

q: What mineral content is ideal for brewing specialty coffee?
opts: Pure distilled water (0 minerals)
Low-medium mineral content, especially magnesium — around 150ppm total
Heavily mineralised water for maximum extraction
Softened water with no calcium or magnesium
explain: Magnesium ions are particularly effective at binding to coffee flavour compounds and extracting them. Too few minerals = flat extraction. Too many = scale buildup and over-extraction. Prague tap water is actually quite good for coffee brewing at ~150–200ppm.

q: What is 'pre-infusion' in espresso machines?
opts: Pre-heating the portafilter before loading coffee
Wetting the puck at low pressure before full extraction pressure
Rinsing the group head before each shot
Soaking beans in water before grinding
explain: Pre-infusion gradually wets the puck at low pressure before ramping to full brewing pressure. This allows the puck to swell evenly, reduces channeling, and can improve extraction uniformity. Your Quick Mill Pop-Up has programmable pre-infusion.

q: What happens if your bloom water amount is too small in pour over?
opts: Nothing — bloom amount doesn't matter
Insufficient CO₂ release — remaining gas disrupts the main extraction
The coffee over-extracts
The filter paper floats up
explain: Using less than 2× coffee weight for the bloom means not all CO₂ is released. When you add the main brew water, remaining CO₂ still escapes, creating uneven flow and potentially channeling. Use at least 2–3× coffee weight for bloom.

q: What is 'bypass' in filter coffee?
opts: Water that bypasses the paper filter
Water that flows through the coffee bed without extracting properly
Adding hot water to a concentrated brew to dilute to desired strength
The overflow channel in a dripper
explain: Bypass dilution means brewing a more concentrated coffee and adding hot water afterward to reach target strength. This gives more control over strength without affecting extraction yield. Common in batch brewing and some pour over recipes.

q: What is 'fines migration' and why does it matter?
opts: Fine coffee particles moving through the filter into the cup
Tiny coffee particles migrating to the bottom of the puck, restricting flow as extraction progresses
The movement of dissolved compounds during extraction
Fine particles evaporating during roasting
explain: During espresso extraction, the finest particles (fines) migrate through the puck toward the bottom, progressively restricting water flow. This naturally slows the shot as it progresses — which is actually desirable as it prevents over-extraction at the end.

q: What is thermal stability in an espresso machine and why does it matter?
opts: The machine's ability to stay cool during use
Consistent, stable group head temperature throughout the shot — critical for shot-to-shot consistency
How quickly the machine heats up from cold
The insulation of the steam wand
explain: Temperature fluctuations during extraction change extraction rate mid-shot, producing inconsistent results. Your GaggiaMate PID upgrade on the Gaggia Classic solves this — PID controllers maintain temperature within 0.5°C, dramatically improving consistency.

q: In pour over, why do longer gaps between pours sometimes improve the cup?
opts: Longer gaps cool the brew water to optimal temperature
More contact time between water and grounds increases extraction, adding body
Shorter gaps produce better results — longer gaps are never better
Longer gaps allow the paper filter to swell properly
explain: Longer gaps between pours extend total brew time and allow more extraction from each addition of water. For under-extracted cups, extending gaps can add body and sweetness. Your 40–50 second gaps between pours are well-calibrated for light washed coffees.

q: What is the purpose of a refractometer in coffee brewing?
opts: Measuring grind particle size
Measuring the TDS (strength) of brewed coffee to calculate extraction yield
Measuring water temperature
Measuring espresso flow rate
explain: A refractometer measures how light bends through the brew, which correlates to dissolved solids concentration (TDS). Combined with brew ratio, you can calculate extraction yield. This lets you dial in objectively rather than purely by taste.

q: What is 'Turkish grind' and how fine is it?
opts: Medium-fine, like table salt
Extremely fine, finer than espresso — almost powder
Medium, like granulated sugar
Coarse, like sea salt
explain: Turkish grind is the finest setting on most grinders — almost talcum powder consistency. It's used for Turkish/Greek coffee prepared in a cezve (ibrik), where grounds steep directly in water at just below boiling point without any filtering.

q: What is pressure profiling in espresso?
opts: Measuring pressure to know when the shot is done
Varying pump pressure throughout extraction to manipulate flavour development
Setting the machine to a fixed pressure for all shots
A technique for milk steaming
explain: Pressure profiling varies water pressure during extraction — typically starting low for even pre-infusion, ramping to full pressure, then declining at the end. Your Quick Mill Pop-Up supports this, allowing you to experiment with extraction profiles for different coffees.

q: Why is a flat burr grinder generally preferred for espresso over a conical burr?
opts: Flat burrs are cheaper
Flat burrs produce a more bimodal particle distribution with better separation of fines and coarses
Flat burrs are quieter
Flat burrs retain less heat
explain: Flat burrs like your Timemore Sculptor 064S and Eureka Zenith 65E produce a narrower, more uniform particle size distribution. This means more even extraction — fewer very fine particles causing bitterness and fewer coarse particles causing sourness.

q: What is shot 'blonding' in espresso?
opts: Adding milk to espresso
The point where espresso colour transitions from dark amber/brown to pale yellow — indicating the shot is finishing
A roasting technique that produces lighter coloured beans
Crema formation at the start of extraction
explain: As espresso extracts, the colour gradually lightens. Blonding is when it goes from rich amber to pale/white — indicating the extractable compounds are mostly gone and the shot is becoming bitter and watery. Many baristas cut the shot at first blond to preserve sweetness.

q: Why should you purge the group head before pulling a shot?
opts: To cool the machine down
To flush stale water sitting in the group head and stabilise temperature
To clean the shower screen
To build pressure before the shot
explain: Water sitting in the group head absorbs heat and can fluctuate in temperature. A 2–3 second purge flushes this water and stabilises group head temperature before extraction. Essential for temperature-sensitive light roasts.

q: What is 'EY' in coffee tasting/analysis?
opts: Extraction Yield — percentage of coffee mass dissolved into the cup
Expected Yield — target amount of espresso output
Enzyme Yield — amount of active enzymes in the brew
Extraction Year — vintage of the coffee crop
explain: Extraction Yield (EY) is the percentage of dry coffee mass that ended up in your cup. Calculated as: (brew weight × TDS) / dry coffee weight × 100. SCA recommends 18–22% for optimal flavour. Below 18% = under-extracted. Above 22% = over-extracted.

q: What causes espresso to have channeling despite careful puck preparation?
opts: Only incorrect tamping causes channeling
Stale beans
Multiple causes: uneven distribution, clumped grounds, cracked puck, worn basket, over-tamping edges
High extraction temperature only
explain: Channeling has many causes: clumped grounds (WDT helps), uneven tamping, a puck cracked by over-aggressive pre-infusion, worn portafilter basket holes, too-fine grind creating cracks, or even a slightly tilted tamp. This is why consistent puck prep matters so much.

q: As coffee ages on the shelf after roasting, what happens to its pour over extraction?
opts: It becomes harder to extract, requiring finer grind
It becomes easier to over-extract, often requiring coarser grind
Aging has no effect on extraction
It requires lower temperature
explain: Fresh coffee resists water penetration (CO₂ outgassing creates pressure). As coffee ages, CO₂ dissipates, the cell structure degrades, and coffee becomes more porous — extracting faster and more easily. You may need to grind coarser or reduce time as the bag ages.

q: What is burr seasoning (break-in) and why does it matter?
opts: Cleaning burrs with a special solution
Running coffee through new burrs to smooth microscopic rough spots, changing grind consistency
Oiling burr grinders to prevent rust
Adjusting burr alignment after installation
explain: New burrs have microscopic rough spots from manufacturing. Running 1–5kg of coffee through breaks these in, producing a more consistent, even cut. This is why your Sculptor 064S shifted zones — the burrs became more efficient, requiring finer settings for espresso and coarser for filter.

q: What is the correct way to rinse a paper filter in pour over?
opts: No rinsing needed — it changes the flavour
Rinse with cold water only
Rinse with hot water to remove paper taste and pre-heat the dripper
Soak in coffee water from the previous brew
explain: Paper filters can impart a papery taste. Rinsing with hot water removes this and pre-heats the dripper (important for temperature stability). Always discard the rinse water before adding coffee. Some premium filters (Chemex, Origami) especially benefit from rinsing.

q: In espresso, what does 'lungo' mean?
opts: A short, concentrated shot
A long shot with more water, typically 1:3–1:4 ratio
A shot with milk added
A shot extracted at lower temperature
explain: Lungo (Italian: long) uses more water than standard espresso — typically 60–90ml vs 30ml. The 1:3–1:4 ratio produces a larger, more dilute, sometimes more bitter shot. Different from Americano (espresso + hot water added after extraction).

q: Why do light roast espressos often need higher temperature than medium roasts?
opts: Light roasts are denser and require more energy
Light roasts have more complex compounds that only dissolve efficiently at higher temperatures
Lighter roasts produce less crema at lower temperatures
Higher temperature compensates for finer grind needed
explain: Lightly roasted beans have more intact cell structure and complex sugars that require more thermal energy to dissolve. At 90°C a light roast may taste sour and thin; at 94–96°C the same coffee opens up with sweetness and complexity. This is why your 90–92°C range suits your light roasts.

q: What is the purpose of a bottomless (naked) portafilter?
opts: Aesthetic — just looks professional
Reveals channeling and puck defects in real time, helping diagnose extraction issues
Produces better espresso than a spouted portafilter
Allows larger doses of coffee
explain: A bottomless portafilter has no spout — espresso drips directly from the basket. This lets you see exactly where water is coming through. Uneven dripping, spraying, or channeling is immediately visible. You use one on both your home and office setups.

q: What is the key principle behind freezing whole bean coffee?
opts: Freezing improves flavour by continuing fermentation
Freezing slows oxidation and cell degradation to ~5% of room temperature rate
Freezing locks in moisture for a better bloom
Freezing prevents mould only, with no effect on freshness
explain: At freezing temperatures, oxidation and enzymatic degradation essentially stop. Coffee ages at roughly 5% of normal rate when frozen — 1 month frozen ≈ 1.5 days at room temperature. Take out only what you need, allow to reach room temperature sealed before opening to prevent condensation.

q: What is 'grind retention' and why is it a problem?
opts: Coffee grounds retained in the mouth after drinking
Old grounds retained inside the grinder mixed with fresh grounds, affecting flavour
The maximum dose a grinder can hold
Grounds sticking to the portafilter basket
explain: Grind retention is old coffee stuck in the grinder's burr chamber or chute from previous sessions. This stale coffee mixes with fresh grounds, degrading flavour. Single-dose grinders like your Sculptor 064S minimise this. Purge a few grams before dialing in a new coffee.

q: Which coffee in your collection is the only one you rated 5★?
opts: Ethiopia Iron Lion — Kmen
Beskydská směs Zimní — Upraženo
Costa Rica San Ramon de Altura — DOT Coffee
Mia Costa Rica Cerro San Luis
explain: Costa Rica San Ramon de Altura from DOT Coffee — 5★, My Pick, Would Buy Again. White tea, floral, honey, funky notes. 88°C, 50g bloom, 4 pour phases, 320g total. Your best pour over to date.

q: Which origin appears in your highest-rated coffees for both espresso AND pour over?
opts: Ethiopia
Kenya
Colombia
Costa Rica
explain: Costa Rica. DOT Coffee San Ramon (5★ pour over) and Mia Cerro San Luis (4★ espresso) are both Costa Rican. It's your most consistently excellent origin across both brew methods.

q: Your Timemore Sculptor 064S espresso zone shifted as the burrs seasoned. Which direction?
opts: Coarser — from ~5.5 to ~7.5 clicks
Finer — from ~5.5 to ~3.5 clicks
The zone didn't change — only the flavour improved
Coarser — from ~3.5 to ~5.5 clicks
explain: As flat burrs season, they cut more efficiently and require finer settings for the same extraction. Your espresso zone shifted ~1.5–2 clicks finer over the first months. This is normal and expected — and is why early entries are marked as Early Entry.

q: What coffee do you currently have in the freezer that was your 'first espresso that truly wowed' you?
opts: Ethiopia Iron Lion — Kmen
BONZO local blend
Tarbon A.I
Master Vietnam Espresso
explain: Tarbon A.I — your first truly impressive espresso. First bag: banana, tropical fruit, fluid mouthfeel. Required many shots to dial in but worth it. Second bag was unfortunately entirely different and undialable. You keep the frozen portion as a reminder of what's possible.

q: You used an unusually low temperature for your best-rated pour over. What was it for the DOT Coffee Costa Rica?
explain: 88°C — lower than your usual 91–92°C. This preserved the delicate white tea, floral and honey notes that would have been over-extracted at higher temperatures. The intentional temperature drop was key to the 5★ result.

q: Which grinder do you use for both espresso AND pour over at home?
opts: Eureka Zenith 65E
Baratza Encore
Timemore Sculptor 064S
Baratza Vario
explain: The Timemore Sculptor 064S is your home workhorse — 64mm flat burrs, stepless adjustment, used for both home espresso (zone ~3.5–4.5) and home pour over (zone ~15). The wide adjustment range is one of its key features.

q: Your office espresso machine received a significant upgrade. What was it?
opts: New portafilter baskets
IMS shower screen
GaggiaMate PID temperature controller
Upgraded pump
explain: GaggiaMate — a PID temperature controller upgrade for the Gaggia Classic Evo Pro. This adds precise temperature control and stability that the stock Gaggia lacks. It dramatically improves shot-to-shot consistency by eliminating temperature surges.

q: What pour over phases do you use for most of your V60 brews?
opts: 2 phases — bloom + one main pour
3 phases — bloom + two pours
4 phases — bloom + three pours
5 phases — bloom + four pours
explain: 4 phases: bloom (40–50g) + three progressive pours building to target weight. Typical timing: bloom 0:00, pour 2 at 0:45, pour 3 at 1:30, pour 4 at 2:10. Target total time 3:00–3:35 depending on coffee.

q: Which Czech roastery is your permanent office espresso anchor coffee from?
opts: Kmen Coffee Roasters
Mia Coffee
Upraženo
Chroast
explain: Chroast from Hradec Králové — 'Kafe, protoze koks je drahej' blend. 50% Colombia + 50% Brazil, light roast. Apple, chocolate, caramel. Easy to dial (grind 1.75 on Eureka), stable, forgiving. 1kg bags for the office.

q: Which seasonal winter blend from Upraženo earned a 4★ and My Pick badge?
opts: Colombia Huitaca
Brazil Sul de Minas
Beskydská směs – Zimní edice
Tarbon A.I
explain: Beskydská směs Zimní edice — seasonal winter espresso blend from Upraženo (Beskydy mountains). Complex, comforting, sweet. Great in milk drinks. 4★, My Pick, Would Buy Again. A rare case of a blend earning My Pick status.

q: What was the grind setting on your Timemore Sculptor for Ethiopia Iron Lion espresso?
explain: Grind 5.9, 18g in / 40g out (1:2.22 ratio), 28s at 92°C. The 40g yield was critical — 36g was too bitter, 41.6g too flat. A good example of how precise yield targeting matters for naturals.

q: What problematic coffee is still waiting in the freezer for a second dial-in attempt?
opts: BONZO local blend
Ethiopia Iron Lion
Master Vietnam Espresso
Tarbon A.I
explain: Master Vietnam Espresso — your earliest coffee, dialed when the grinder was still unseasoned. The first attempt was unreliable. With the Sculptor now fully seasoned and your skills improved, the frozen second bag is waiting for a proper dial-in session.

q: What are the roaster-stated flavour notes of Ethiopia Iron Lion from Kmen?
opts: Jasmine, bergamot, white grapes
Banana, tropical fruit, panela
Plums, lemon sorbet, green tea
Blackcurrant, rhubarb, honey
explain: Plums, lemon sorbet, green tea — from the label you photographed. Natural process light roast for espresso. Noticably different from Kmen's Chelbesa (washed, jasmine/bergamot). The natural process produces that heavier, plum-forward profile.

q: What ratio do you use for home V60 pour over brewing?
explain: 1:15 — 20g coffee to 300g water. This produces a concentrated, flavourful cup that suits your preference for light washed coffees where you want to taste the complexity clearly without excessive dilution.

q: Which roaster produced your highest-rated and only 5★ coffee?
opts: Kmen Coffee Roasters
Mia Coffee
Pikolo
DOT Coffee
explain: DOT Coffee — Costa Rica San Ramon de Altura. DOT is a Czech specialty roaster focused on transparency and quality sourcing. The San Ramon was exceptional — white tea, floral, honey character at 88°C. Worth seeking out again.

q: What is 'terroir' in the context of specialty coffee?
opts: The roasting technique
The collective influence of soil, altitude, climate and geography on flavour
The variety of coffee plant grown
The processing method used after harvest
explain: Borrowed from wine, terroir describes how place shapes flavour. Ethiopian Chelbesa at 2000m altitude with volcanic soil, specific rainfall patterns, and heirloom varieties produces jasmine and bergamot — not because it's Ethiopia but because of that exact microclimate.

q: What is the 'SCA Flavour Wheel' used for?
opts: Choosing coffee for purchase
A standardised vocabulary for describing coffee flavours, from basic categories to specific notes
Measuring extraction yield
Training baristas on milk steaming
explain: The SCA Flavour Wheel (co-developed with World Coffee Research) gives a common language for coffee flavour. It starts broadly (Fruity, Floral, Sweet, Nutty/Cocoa, Spices, Roasted, Other) and drills to specific descriptors like 'blackcurrant', 'jasmine', 'caramel'.

q: Which country is the world's largest coffee producer?
opts: Colombia
Vietnam
Ethiopia
Brazil
explain: Brazil produces ~40% of the world's coffee — by far the most. It grows mostly Arabica and some Robusta, predominantly natural/pulped natural processed. Vietnam is second (mostly Robusta). Colombia is third (famous for quality over quantity).

q: What made Panama Gesha coffee become so famous and expensive?
opts: It contains the most caffeine of any coffee
Hacienda La Esmeralda's 2004 auction revealed its extraordinary jasmine, bergamot, tropical fruit complexity
It was the first certified organic coffee
It grows only at the highest altitudes in the world
explain: Hacienda La Esmeralda entered their Gesha in the 2004 Best of Panama auction and it sold for $21/lb — unprecedented at the time. The tea-like, perfumed, jasmine-heavy character was unlike anything tasters had experienced. It redefined what coffee could taste like.

q: What is the difference between Arabica and Robusta at the genetic level?
opts: They are the same species with different processing
Arabica is tetraploid (4 sets of chromosomes), Robusta diploid (2 sets)
Robusta has been genetically modified
They are actually the same species
explain: Arabica (Coffea arabica) is a tetraploid — it contains 4 sets of chromosomes, giving it greater genetic complexity and the potential for complex flavours. Robusta (Coffea canephora) is diploid. This genetic difference underlies their flavour complexity difference.

q: What is 'direct sunlight' drying vs 'raised bed' drying in coffee processing?
opts: No difference — both are sun drying
Raised beds (African beds) allow airflow under the beans, more even drying, better quality
Direct sunlight is better as more UV improves flavour
Raised beds are used only for natural, direct for washed
explain: African raised beds (wire mesh tables) allow air circulation above and below the beans. This produces more even drying, less fermentation risk, and better quality than drying on patios or tarps. Your Pikolo Kenya and most top Ethiopian coffees use raised bed drying.

q: What does 'AA' grade mean for Kenyan coffee?
opts: AA = processed by anaerobic method
AA = top screen size grade (6.8mm+) indicating large, dense beans
AA = aged over 2 years
AA = 100% single farm, no blending
explain: Kenyan coffee is graded by screen size. AA is the top grade (17+ screen, 6.8mm+ bean diameter). AB, PB (peaberry), C follow. Larger beans from high altitude tend to be denser with more complex flavours — though grade alone doesn't guarantee taste quality.

q: What is the 'Speciality Coffee Association' (SCA)?
opts: A government body regulating coffee quality
An international non-profit organisation that sets standards and promotes specialty coffee quality
A certification for baristas only
A trade union for coffee farmers
explain: The SCA is the world's largest coffee trade association — setting quality standards, cupping protocols, brewing guidelines, and certifying baristas and Q graders. Their research and education has shaped modern specialty coffee culture globally.

q: What is a Q grader?
opts: A grading machine for sorting coffee beans
A certified professional trained to objectively evaluate and score coffee using SCA protocols
A barista certification level
A person who grades espresso machines
explain: Q graders are certified by the Coffee Quality Institute — the most rigorous coffee professional certification. They're trained to score coffees using SCA cupping protocols on a 100-point scale. Their assessments determine whether a coffee qualifies as specialty grade.

q: What role do yeasts and bacteria play in coffee fermentation during processing?
opts: They only cause negative defects and must be eliminated
They break down the mucilage (fruit layer) and create flavour compounds that transfer into the bean
They primarily affect caffeine content
They have no role — only enzymes matter
explain: During fermentation (especially in washed and anaerobic processing), yeasts and bacteria consume the sugars in the mucilage, producing alcohols, acids and esters that transfer into the bean. Controlled fermentation is an art — different microbe populations produce different flavour profiles.

q: Brazil is famous for producing large quantities of which flavour profile?
opts: Bright, floral, jasmine-forward
Nutty, chocolate, low acidity, full body
Fruity, wine-like, heavy fermented notes
Tea-like, bergamot, extremely clean
explain: Brazilian coffee is typically known for chocolate, nuts, caramel, low acidity, and full body. The lower altitude, natural/pulped natural processing, and Bourbon/Catuai varieties produce this approachable, sweet, crowd-pleasing profile. Perfect for blends and espresso.

q: What does 'farm gate price' mean in specialty coffee?
opts: The price paid at the supermarket gate
The price paid directly to the farmer at their farm — as opposed to FOB (port) or import prices
The total cost including transport to the customer
The minimum price set by governments
explain: Farm gate price is what farmers actually receive for their coffee — often very different from retail prices. Specialty coffee emphasises transparency about farm gate prices. Many direct trade roasters publish the prices they pay to demonstrate they're supporting sustainable farming incomes.

q: Wine and coffee share a concept of 'vintage' — what does a coffee vintage mean?
opts: The year the coffee variety was developed
The crop year the coffee was harvested — which affects flavour just as wine vintages do
The year the roastery was founded
The age of the roasting equipment used
explain: Coffee is seasonal — each crop is harvested once a year and its character varies with that year's weather, rainfall, and ripeness. A 2024 crop from the same farm as 2023 may taste different. Some enthusiasts track vintages, especially for exceptional coffees.

q: What is the approximate caffeine content of a 250ml filter coffee?
explain: A standard 250ml filter coffee contains roughly 80–150mg caffeine. Interestingly, this is often MORE caffeine than a double espresso (~80–120mg) despite espresso tasting stronger — because filter uses more total water to extract from.

q: What is 'staling' in roasted coffee?
opts: Mould growth on beans
Oxidation and off-gassing of flavour compounds, causing loss of aroma and complexity over time
Over-fermentation during processing
Staleness from too-dark roasting
explain: After roasting, coffee continuously loses volatile aromatic compounds through oxidation and off-gassing. Staling produces flat, cardboard-like flavours. Specialty coffee recommends consuming within 2–4 weeks of roast for filter, 1–3 weeks for espresso (though your light roast espressos benefit from 1–2 weeks rest first).

q: What is a 'natural' or 'dry processed' coffee?
opts: Coffee grown without pesticides
The entire cherry is dried with the fruit intact before hulling
Coffee that has not been roasted
Coffee grown in its natural habitat without farming
explain: Natural process = drying the whole cherry intact. After drying (3–6 weeks on raised beds), the dried fruit is mechanically removed. The extended contact with the fruit imparts fruity, wine-like, heavy-body characteristics. Ethiopia's Harrar coffees are classic dry-processed examples.

q: What is the significance of 'cherry ripeness' in specialty coffee quality?
opts: Ripeness doesn't matter — processing determines quality
Ripe cherries have fully developed sugars and acids; unripe = astringency, overripe = fermentation defects
Greener cherries produce lighter roasts
Overripe cherries always produce better natural process coffees
explain: Cherry ripeness is fundamental to cup quality. Ripe red/yellow cherries have fully developed sugars, optimal moisture content, and peak flavour potential. Under-ripe = astringent, tannic, harsh. Over-ripe = fermented, rotting notes. Hand-picking ripe cherries only is what separates specialty from commodity.

q: What does 'lot' mean in specialty coffee purchasing?
opts: A specific quantity unit (1 lot = 1 tonne)
A specific batch of coffee traceable to a harvest time, farm section, or processing batch
A grading term for defect percentage
The total annual production of a farm
explain: A lot is a specific, traceable batch of coffee — often identified by processing date, farm section, or variety. Specialty roasters buy specific lots (e.g. 'Kmen Chelbesa Lot 12, natural, June 2025 harvest') rather than generic origin. Lot transparency allows consistent quality tracking.

q: What is crema in espresso and what makes it?
opts: Milk foam mixed into the espresso
An emulsion of CO₂ gas, coffee oils and water proteins — formed by high-pressure extraction
Burned coffee residue that floats on top
Caramelised sugars produced by high temperature
explain: Crema forms when high pressure (9 bar) forces CO₂ out of solution with coffee oils, creating a stable emulsion. It carries aromatic compounds and contributes to mouthfeel. Crema quality indicates freshness — very fresh coffee produces thick, dark crema; stale coffee produces thin, pale crema.

q: What is 'sourcing transparency' in specialty coffee?
opts: Publishing the price of the final product
Publishing information about where the coffee comes from — farm, farmer, altitude, variety, price paid
Transparent packaging that shows the beans
Transparency about roasting temperatures
explain: Sourcing transparency means roasters publishing detailed information about their supply chain: which farm, which farmer, altitude, processing method, and ideally the price paid to the farmer. This accountability drives quality improvement and fair compensation throughout the supply chain.

q: What role does salt play in coffee flavour perception?
opts: Salt ruins coffee — never add it
Small amounts of sodium in water or occasionally added to grounds suppress bitterness and enhance sweetness
Salt increases caffeine extraction
Salt is a defect indicator from sea-level grown coffees
explain: Sodium ions suppress bitter taste receptors — this is why some people add a tiny pinch of salt to bitter coffee. Your brew water also naturally contains some sodium. In brewing water, the balance of calcium, magnesium, sodium and bicarbonate all affect perceived flavour.

q: What is 'over-agitation' in pour over and what can it cause?
opts: There is no such thing as over-agitation
Excessive stirring or turbulence can increase extraction speed to the point of over-extraction and muddy the cup
Over-agitation only affects brew time, not flavour
Agitation has no effect — only water amount matters
explain: While agitation generally helps extraction, excessive agitation can disrupt the coffee bed structure, increase fines migration through the filter, and potentially over-extract. More agitation = faster extraction — great for under-extracted cups, but too much with a fully dialed coffee can introduce bitterness.

q: What is the 'golden ratio' for espresso most specialty baristas target?
opts: 1:1 (ristretto)
explain: 1:2 to 1:2.5 is the modern specialty espresso sweet spot — 18g dose producing 36–45g espresso. This balances sweetness, body, and acidity. Ristretto (1:1.5) is more intense and sweet. Lungo (1:3+) starts to dilute and can introduce bitterness.

q: How do you calculate the brew ratio for filter coffee?
opts: Brew ratio = brew time ÷ coffee dose
Brew ratio = water weight ÷ coffee weight (e.g. 300g ÷ 20g = 15, expressed as 1:15)
Brew ratio = coffee dose ÷ water temperature
Brew ratio = extraction yield ÷ TDS
explain: Brew ratio = water weight ÷ coffee weight. 300g water with 20g coffee = 1:15. This is the single most important variable to control in filter brewing for consistency. A 1:15 ratio with 20g = 300g water; with 25g = 375g water. Always dose by weight, not volume.

q: What is the ideal water temperature for French Press brewing?
opts: 100°C (boiling)
explain: 90–96°C works well for French Press — same as filter coffee. Some recommend slightly cooler (88–92°C) for light roasts to prevent over-extraction during the long steep time (4 minutes). French Press extracts slowly through immersion, so temperature control is less critical than in pour over.

q: Why does grind size for espresso need to be much finer than for filter coffee?
opts: Espresso uses less water so needs more surface area
Espresso brewing is extremely fast (25–30 seconds) so needs maximum surface area for quick extraction
Filter coffee can only use coarser grinds due to paper filter pore size
Finer grind improves crema formation only
explain: Espresso has only 25–35 seconds of contact time at high pressure. To achieve full extraction in this time, extremely fine grind provides maximum surface area. Filter coffee brews for 3–5 minutes, allowing coarser grinds to extract fully over longer contact time.

q: What is the 'bypass' method and when might you use it?
opts: Bypassing the bloom phase for faster brewing
Brewing a concentrated coffee and adding hot water to reach target strength — useful for controlling strength without affecting extraction
Using bypass water channels in the espresso machine
A method of bypassing paper filter clogging
explain: Brew bypass = make a concentrated coffee at a specific extraction yield, then add hot water to dilute to drinking strength. This decouples strength (how much coffee you taste) from extraction (how much of the bean you extracted). Useful for batch brewing or dialing in difficult coffees.

q: In pour over, what does a very fast drawdown time typically indicate?
opts: Perfect grind size
Coffee is too coarse — water flows through too quickly, under-extracting
Coffee is too fresh — CO₂ is repelling water
The filter paper has a defect
explain: Fast drawdown (coffee empties the dripper quickly) means water is flowing through without enough resistance — too coarse grind or insufficient coffee dose. This produces under-extracted, thin, sour coffee. Grind finer or increase dose to slow drawdown and improve extraction.

q: What is 'stalling' in pour over and when does it happen?
opts: The timer stopping during brewing
The coffee bed getting clogged — drawdown slows to almost nothing
When the bloom phase takes too long
When water temperature drops below 85°C
explain: Stalling happens when fine grounds, too-fine grind, or overworked grounds clog the filter and slow drawdown to a crawl or stop. This causes over-extraction of whatever water remains in contact. Prevention: slightly coarser grind, avoid over-agitation, quality filters.

q: What does 'on the fly adjustment' mean in espresso dialing?
opts: Adjusting the grinder between shots to correct extraction
Using a mobile app to control the espresso machine
Adjusting milk temperature while steaming
Making dose adjustments mid-shot
explain: On the fly adjustment means changing grind settings between shots in real time to move toward target extraction. If shot is 25 seconds at target yield but tastes sour — go finer. If 35 seconds — go coarser. The goal is finding the window where the shot tastes balanced, not just hitting time targets.

q: What are 'heirloom' coffee varieties in Ethiopia?
opts: Old roasting equipment still in use
Indigenous Ethiopian wild varieties that haven't been selectively bred — producing enormous genetic diversity
Coffees from the oldest farms in Ethiopia
Coffee varieties that have been imported to Ethiopia
explain: Ethiopian heirlooms are the diverse, unselected wild varieties that evolved over millennia in Ethiopia's forests. Unlike named cultivars (Typica, Bourbon, Gesha), they're simply called 'heirloom' — and their genetic diversity produces the extraordinary range of flavours in Ethiopian coffees.

q: What is 'shade grown' coffee and why do some consider it superior?
opts: Coffee grown under artificial lighting
Coffee grown under forest canopy — slower growth, more complex flavours, better for biodiversity
Coffee dried in the shade rather than sunlight
Low-altitude coffee not exposed to direct mountain sunlight
explain: Shade-grown coffee matures more slowly under forest canopy, allowing more complex flavour development. It also supports biodiversity (birds, insects, soil health) versus sun-grown monocultures. Many specialty coffees from Ethiopia and Central America are naturally shade-grown.

q: What causes 'baked' flavour in coffee (flat, bready, lifeless)?
opts: Over-extraction during brewing
Stalling during roasting at a critical temperature range — typically 160–170°C — without enough energy
Under-extraction during brewing
Using old water for brewing
explain: 'Baked' coffees result from a roast that stalled in development — insufficient heat in the drum caused the bean to cook slowly without Maillard reaction proceeding properly. This degrades aromatic compounds, leaving a flat, bread-like, lifeless cup. Common with home roasters that lack drum control.

q: What is a 'cupping' and how does it work?
opts: A medical practice for coffee farmers
A standardised tasting protocol: coarsely ground coffee steeped in hot water, then tasted by breaking the crust and slurping
A method of measuring cup volume for extraction yield
A technique for identifying coffee defects by smell only
explain: Cupping is the universal evaluation protocol. Coarse grounds in a bowl, hot water poured over, 4-minute steep, crust broken and aroma assessed, then spoonfuls slurped (to spray across all taste receptors). The SCA cupping form scores fragrance, aroma, flavour, aftertaste, acidity, body, balance, uniformity, clean cup, and sweetness.

q: What is 'green coffee' in the coffee trade?
opts: Unripe coffee cherries still on the plant
Unroasted coffee beans — the raw commodity traded globally before roasting
Coffee blended with matcha
Organic, eco-certified coffee
explain: Green coffee = unroasted beans. After processing (removing fruit, drying, hulling), the raw beans are the commodity traded globally. Roasters import green coffee and roast to order. Green coffee can be stored for 1–2 years with careful storage, unlike roasted which stales within weeks.

q: What is the SCA colour scale used for measuring roast level?
opts: Pantone numbers for visual matching
Agtron scale — a numerical measurement of how much light the bean reflects
A visual chart with photos of 8 roast levels
RGB colour codes matched to roast
explain: The Agtron scale measures light reflectance — lighter beans reflect more. Agtron 95 = very light roast. Agtron 35 = dark roast. The Specialty Coffee Association uses Agtron tiles as reference. This objective measurement removes subjectivity from roast level descriptions.

q: What makes Ethiopian natural process coffees from Yirgacheffe so unusual?
opts: They contain the most caffeine of any coffee
The combination of floral heirloom varieties + natural processing produces blueberry, strawberry, jasmine notes unlike anywhere else
They are always decaffeinated by law
The altitude is too high for other processes
explain: Yirgacheffe naturals (Harfusa, Beloya, etc.) combine heirloom genetic diversity with natural processing — the fruit ferments onto the bean for weeks. The result is often intensely blueberry, strawberry, jasmine — simultaneously fruity and floral. Unique in the coffee world.

q: What is 'varietals' in coffee and why do they matter?
opts: The packaging variety options available
Specific subspecies/cultivars of Coffea arabica with distinct flavour characteristics
The variety of roast levels available from one farm
Different processing methods applied to the same bean
explain: Coffee varietals are cultivars of Arabica with distinct genetics. Gesha = jasmine/bergamot. SL28 = blackcurrant/bright acidity. Bourbon = round sweetness. Typica = classic clean. Just as grape variety determines wine character, coffee variety fundamentally shapes flavour — same origin, different varietal = very different cup.

q: What is 'batch brew' coffee?
opts: Brewing multiple espresso shots at once
Filter coffee brewed in large quantities (1–12L) in commercial filter machines, kept warm in a thermal carafe
Coffee brewed in batches of pour over cones simultaneously
A cold brew technique using multiple batches
explain: Batch brew is commercial-scale filter coffee — large machine brews a full carafe at once. High-quality batch brewers (like Bunn, Marco, Moccamaster) can produce excellent coffee at consistent quality. Third-wave cafes use batch brew for high-volume filter service alongside individual pour overs.

q: What is the 'wet mill' in coffee processing?
opts: A machine that adds water to roasted beans
The facility where coffee cherries are processed — pulping, fermentation, washing, and initial drying
The grinder used for wet grinding espresso
A method of brewing with cold water
explain: The wet mill (or washing station) is where freshly picked cherries go for processing — pulping removes the fruit skin, fermentation tanks remove mucilage, washing channels clean the beans, and raised beds begin drying. The quality of wet mill processing fundamentally determines cup quality.

q: What is 'dial-in' in espresso and why is it necessary?
opts: Setting the machine's digital timer
The process of adjusting grind, dose, and yield to find the optimal extraction parameters for a specific coffee
Programming the espresso machine's pressure profile
Calibrating the espresso machine's temperature controller
explain: Dialing in means finding the specific grind size, dose, and yield that makes a particular coffee taste its best. Every new bag requires dialing — different density, freshness, and processing all change optimal parameters. This iterative process (pull shot → taste → adjust) is central to specialty espresso.

q: What is 'musical extraction' or 'sweet spot' in espresso?
opts: Playing music during extraction improves flavour
The narrow range of extraction variables where a coffee tastes balanced and delicious
A metaphor for perfectly timed milk steaming
The sound the espresso machine makes during optimal extraction
explain: The sweet spot is the extraction window where a coffee tastes best — correctly extracted with balanced sweetness, acidity, and bitterness. Outside this window: too sour (under), too bitter (over). The sweet spot varies per coffee — tight for complex single origins, wider for forgiving blends.

q: Why do some baristas use a puck screen in espresso?
opts: To add filtration above the coffee puck
To distribute water evenly across the puck surface and prevent the shower screen from imprinting the puck
To measure the exact volume of espresso
To prevent coffee from entering the group head
explain: A metal puck screen sits on top of the coffee puck before locking in the portafilter. It distributes water more evenly from the shower screen and prevents the shower screen imprint (which can cause channeling). Also makes cleanup easier. You use a 58mm puck screen on your office setup.

q: Why should you always tare (zero) your scale after placing the portafilter?
opts: To measure the portafilter weight separately
To measure only the coffee dose without the portafilter weight affecting your measurement
The portafilter must be weighed empty first
Scale accuracy requires taring between uses
explain: Portafilters can weigh 300–800g depending on material. Taring after placing the portafilter means your scale reads only the coffee going in — giving you an accurate dose. Similarly, tare the cup under the portafilter to measure only yield, not cup weight.

q: What does 'extraction uniformity' mean and why does it matter?
opts: All shots extracted at the same time of day
Water passing evenly through all parts of the coffee bed, extracting each particle similarly
Using the same grind setting every day
Consistent tamping pressure
explain: Extraction uniformity means every coffee particle extracts to a similar degree. Non-uniform extraction = some particles over-extract (bitter) while others under-extract (sour) — producing a muddled, confused cup. Good distribution, WDT, and puck prep all aim to maximise uniformity.

q: In approximately which month did you start your specialty coffee journey?
opts: January 2025
July 2025
October 2025
March 2026
explain: October 2025 — your earliest logged entry is Brazil Sul de Minas from Drukarnia, October 2025. The grinder was still unseasoning and your technique was developing. Entries from this period are marked 'Early Entry' to reflect the learning context.

q: What espresso machine do you use at home?
opts: Gaggia Classic Evo Pro
La Marzocca Linea Mini
Quick Mill Pop-Up
Breville Barista Express
explain: Quick Mill Pop-Up — single boiler, PID temperature control, and pressure profiling capability. Paired with the Timemore Sculptor 064S. An excellent home espresso setup that allows significant experimentation with pressure and temperature profiles.

q: What grinder do you use at the office for filter/pour over?
opts: Timemore Sculptor 064S
Eureka Zenith 65E
Baratza Encore
Baratza Vario
explain: Baratza Encore — bought used via Aukro. A reliable, simple filter grinder that's appropriate for the office V60. Your espresso at the office uses the Eureka Zenith 65E. The two grinders serve different brew methods.

q: You noted a 'dramatic flavour improvement' on which coffee after correcting your pour over technique?
opts: Ethiopia Chelbesa
Sumatra Jasmine
Kenya Kainamui AA
Costa Rica San Ramon
explain: Kenya Kainamui AA from Pikolo — after switching to slower, higher pours with proper agitation, the flavour dramatically improved. This was a key learning moment showing how technique impacts extraction as much as recipe numbers.

q: Which of your coffees earned both 'My Pick' AND 'Would Buy Again' status?
opts: Tarbon A.I and Ethiopia Iron Lion
Costa Rica San Ramon (DOT) and Beskydská směs Zimní (Upraženo) and Mia Costa Rica
Only the DOT Costa Rica
Ethiopia Iron Lion and Kmen Chelbesa
explain: Three coffees earned both badges: DOT Coffee Costa Rica San Ramon (5★), Upraženo Beskydská směs Zimní (4★), and Mia Coffee Costa Rica Cerro San Luis (4★). Notably both Costa Rican coffees made the list, reinforcing Costa Rica as your top origin.
